intro: While Python prioritizes readability through explicit constructs and a single obvious way to complete tasks, Ruby champions developer happiness through implicit conventions and functional flexibility. Both are dynamic, object-oriented languages frequently leveraged in web frameworks. Programmers transition to Ruby primarily to leverage the opinionated ecosystem of Ruby on Rails or to adopt DSL-driven application design.

diffs.from: Explicit is better than implicit; code readability is paramount.
diffs.to: Developer happiness, high expressiveness, and meta-programming freedom.

diffs.from: Parentheses required for function calls; methods are objects.
diffs.to: Optional parentheses; methods are invocations, blocks are first-class.

diffs.from: Falsy values include None, False, 0, empty collections.
diffs.to: Only false and nil evaluate to falsy; 0 and empty collections are truthy.

diffs.from: Explicit wrapper/decorators or monkey patching via assignment.
diffs.to: Open classes allowing direct extension and runtime modification.

examples.title: Class with Getter/Setter
examples.description: Defining an object structure with basic properties and methods.
examples.toCode:
class User
  attr_accessor :name
  def initialize(name)
    @name = name
  end
end

user = User.new("Bob")
puts user.name

examples.title: Collection Filtering
examples.description: Selecting elements from a collection using a predicate function.
examples.fromCode:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
evens = [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]

tips.title: Check Falsy Logic
tips.body: Verify truthiness conditionals, because 0 and empty arrays ([]) evaluate to true in Ruby.

tips.title: Drop Call Parentheses
tips.body: Omit parentheses on method calls when writing standard idiomatic Ruby, except for complex nested calls.

tips.title: Utilize Open Classes
tips.body: Take advantage of Ruby's open classes cautiously to add utility methods directly to core classes.

tips.title: Leverage attr_accessor
tips.body: Avoid writing manual getter and setter methods by using attr_accessor, attr_reader, or attr_writer.
